evidence.ocr_text:
(i) Prove the identity (1/cos x - tan x)^2 = 1/(1 + sin x). [4]

(ii) Hence solve the equation (1/cos 2x - tan 2x)^2 = 2/3 for 0 <= x <= pi. [5]

evidence.ocr_text:
7 Let I = \int_{0}^{1} \frac{x^5}{(1 + x^2)^3} dx.

(i) Using the substitution u = 1 + x^2, show that I = \int_{1}^{2} \frac{(u - 1)^2}{2u^3} du. [3]

(ii) Hence find the exact value of I. [5]
subquestion_blocks: {'label': '(i)', 'text': 'Using the substitution u = 1 + x^2, show that I = \\int_{1}^{2} \\frac{(u - 1)^2}{2u^3} du.', 'marks': '[3]'}
{'label': '(ii)', 'text': 'Hence find the exact value of I.', 'marks': '[5]'}
layout_hints: Question number 7 at top left
Main definition followed by two sub-parts (i) and (ii)
Marks allocated in square brackets on the right margin
spatial_evidence: Standard vertical layout for a multi-part mathematics problem. The integral definition is at the top, followed by instruction (i) to transform the integral, and instruction (ii) to evaluate it. Marks are aligned to the right of each sub-question.

evidence.ocr_text:
4 Angle x is such that sin x = a + b and cos x = a - b, where a and b are constants.
(i) Show that a^2 + b^2 has a constant value for all values of x. [3]
(ii) In the case where tan x = 2, express a in terms of b. [2]
subquestion_blocks: {'label': '(i)', 'text': 'Show that a^2 + b^2 has a constant value for all values of x.', 'marks': '[3]'}
{'label': '(ii)', 'text': 'In the case where tan x = 2, express a in terms of b.', 'marks': '[2]'}

Dotted lines provided for answers below each sub-question
Mark allocations [3] and [2] aligned to the right of respective sub-questions

evidence.ocr_text:
7 (a) Prove the identity \frac{1 - 2 \sin^2 \theta}{1 - \sin^2 \theta} \equiv 1 - \tan^2 \theta. [2]

(b) Hence solve the equation \frac{1 - 2 \sin^2 \theta}{1 - \sin^2 \theta} = 2 \tan^4 \theta for 0^\circ \leqslant \theta \leqslant 180^\circ. [3]

evidence.ocr_text:
4 (a) Prove the identity \frac{\sin^3 \theta}{\sin \theta - 1} - \frac{\sin^2 \theta}{1 + \sin \theta} \equiv -\tan^2 \theta(1 + \sin^2 \theta). [4]

(b) Hence solve the equation
\frac{\sin^3 \theta}{\sin \theta - 1} - \frac{\sin^2 \theta}{1 + \sin \theta} = \tan^2 \theta(1 - \sin^2 \theta)
for 0 < \theta < 2\pi. [2]

{'label': '(b)', 'text': 'Hence solve the equation \\frac{\\sin^3 \\theta}{\\sin \\theta - 1} - \\frac{\\sin^2 \\theta}{1 + \\sin \\theta} = \\tan^2 \\theta(1 - \\sin^2 \\theta) for 0 < \\theta < 2\\pi.', 'marks': '[2]'}

evidence.ocr_text:
5 (i) Given that 4 tan x + 3 cos x + 1/cos x = 0, show, without using a calculator, that sin x = -1/3. [3]

(ii) Hence, showing all necessary working, solve the equation
4 tan(2x - 20°) + 3 cos(2x - 20°) + 1/cos(2x - 20°) = 0
for 0° ≤ x ≤ 180°. [4]

evidence.ocr_text:
7 Let f(x) = 2 / ((2x - 1)(2x + 1)).
(a) Express f(x) in partial fractions. [2]
(b) Using your answer to part (a), show that
(f(x))^2 = 1 / (2x - 1)^2 - 1 / (2x - 1) + 1 / (2x + 1) + 1 / (2x + 1)^2. [2]
(c) Hence show that ∫_1^2 (f(x))^2 dx = 2/5 + 1/2 ln(5/9). [5]
subquestion_blocks: {'label': '(a)', 'text': 'Express f(x) in partial fractions.', 'marks': '[2]'}
{'label': '(b)', 'text': 'Using your answer to part (a), show that (f(x))^2 = 1/(2x-1)^2 - 1/(2x-1) + 1/(2x+1) + 1/(2x+1)^2.', 'marks': '[2]'}
{'label': '(c)', 'text': 'Hence show that ∫_1^2 (f(x))^2 dx = 2/5 + 1/2 ln(5/9).', 'marks': '[5]'}

(i) Express f(x) in the form A + B/(x + 2) + C/(2x - 1). [4]
(ii) Hence show that ∫_1^? f(x) dx = 6 + 1/2 ln(16/7). [5]

3 (a) Given that cos(x - 30°) = 2 sin(x + 30°), show that tan x = (2 - √3) / (1 - 2√3). [4]

(b) Hence solve the equation

cos(x - 30°) = 2 sin(x + 30°),

for 0° < x < 360°. [2]
